title: Knowledge Management in Projects
summary: Capture, share and reuse tacit and explicit project knowledge so lessons are available at the moment of need. In PMBOK 8 this work is a process within the Governance performance domain supported by the Build an Empowered Culture principle, and the lessons it surfaces feed the Focus on Value principle.

sections.heading: Where knowledge management fits
sections.body: PMBOK 8 pairs six principles with seven performance domains. Managing knowledge is a process within the Governance performance domain - efficient processes and clear communication - and is powered by the Build an Empowered Culture principle: a collaborative, empowered team shares what it knows. Lessons learned are not paperwork; they inform the Focus on Value principle by helping future work make better decisions.

blocks.title: How to use this
lines: Governance domain = the what: keep knowledge flowing so the work runs efficiently.
Build an Empowered Culture principle = the who: collaboration and interaction move know-how person to person.
Focus on Value principle = the why: reused lessons raise the value of future decisions.
The aim is knowledge at the MOMENT of need, not filed and forgotten.

sections.heading: Tacit vs explicit knowledge
sections.body: Managing project knowledge means sharing BOTH kinds. Explicit knowledge moves through repositories and documents; tacit knowledge needs interaction - conversation, pairing, mentoring and observation - because it cannot be fully captured on a page.

groups.label: What it is
lines: Easily written down and stored.
Documents, data, procedures, templates.

groups.label: How it moves
lines: Through repositories and documents.
Copy it, store it, retrieve it.

groups.label: What it is
lines: Personal know-how, experience, judgement, intuition.
Hard to articulate; it lives in people's heads.

groups.label: How it moves
lines: Through interaction, not documents.
Conversation, pairing, mentoring, observation.

blocks.title: Exam cue
blocks.text: If an answer can be copied into a document it is explicit; if it has to be demonstrated or discussed it is tacit.

sections.heading: The DIKW hierarchy
sections.body: Knowledge is built up in stages. Value rises at each level as raw numbers become understanding and, finally, good judgement.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 185' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><polygon points='150,18 36,172 264,172' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.3'/><line x1='121' y1='58' x2='179' y2='58' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.7'/><line x1='92' y1='96' x2='208' y2='96' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.7'/><line x1='64' y1='134' x2='236' y2='134' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.7'/><text x='150' y='45' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Wisdom</text><text x='150' y='81' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Knowledge</text><text x='150' y='118' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Information</text><text x='150' y='156' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Data</text></svg>
figure.caption: DIKW: raw data becomes wisdom as context, experience, and judgement are added.

items.title: Data
lines: Raw measurements and facts with no context.

items.title: Information
lines: Data placed in context so it has meaning.

items.title: Knowledge
lines: Information combined with experience to understand and act.

items.title: Wisdom
lines: Applying knowledge with sound judgement to make good decisions.

blocks.title: Exam cue
blocks.text: Wisdom is knowing which knowledge to apply - and when not to.

sections.heading: Managing project knowledge
sections.body: This work draws on existing organisational knowledge - lessons learned from prior projects - AND creates new knowledge during the current project. Knowledge that is captured but never consulted adds no value.

rows.value: Captures lessons during the project, as they happen.
Rolls up at close into an organisation-wide store for future projects.
Prior organisational knowledge plus new knowledge made on this project.
Make knowledge usable at the moment of need, not filed and forgotten.

blocks.title: Exam cue
blocks.text: Knowledge is an asset only when it reaches the right person at the right time.

sections.heading: Lessons learned vs retrospectives
sections.body: Same goal - learning - but the contrast is cadence and audience. Lessons learned are periodic and outward-facing to the organisation; retrospectives are frequent and inward-facing to the team.

groups.label: When
lines: Captured throughout, and formally at close.

groups.label: Who benefits
lines: The organisation, on future work.

groups.label: When
lines: End of every iteration.

groups.label: Who benefits
lines: This team and its way of working right now.

blocks.title: Exam cue
blocks.text: Retrospectives improve this team this sprint; lessons learned improve future projects.

sections.heading: Transferring knowledge
sections.body: Use methods that move tacit knowledge, not just documents. The usual barrier is behaviour - people simply do not consult what exists - so build knowledge checks into planning and review checkpoints.

rows.value: Groups that share a discipline exchange know-how.
Know-how passes person to person on the job.
Conveys context and reasoning behind decisions.
Usable, findable store for explicit content.

blocks.title: Exam cue
blocks.text: Pull beats push: design the work so people reach for prior knowledge naturally.

sections.heading: Why knowledge is lost and how to keep it
sections.body: Knowledge is lost when it is captured too late, stored where no one can find it, or never rewarded, and when staff leave taking tacit know-how with them.

items.title: Capture at the right moment
lines: Record while the detail is still fresh.

items.title: Make it findable
lines: Well organised and easy to retrieve.

items.title: Recognise sharing
lines: Reward the people who pass knowledge on.

items.title: Build a blameless culture
lines: Psychological safety so honest lessons surface.

blocks.title: Exam cue
blocks.text: No blame, no fear, no hidden lessons - if surfacing a problem invites punishment, the same mistakes repeat.

source: PM Cram study notes - reworded and restructured for the PMBOK 8 principle-based PMP exam.
